Forecasting · SVR · IEEE INCET 2023

Impact of COVID-19 Lockdowns on Air Quality in Bangladesh

Reproducible implementation of the published paper: AQI trends across 10 Bangladeshi cities through pre-COVID, lockdown, and post-lockdown periods, plus SVR-based forecasting (~6.2% MAPE).

PythonScikit-learnSVRTime SeriesForecasting

TIME-SERIES ANALYSIS & FORECASTING

COVID-19 Lockdowns & Air Quality in Bangladesh

Reproducing the analysis from IEEE INCET 2023: per-city AQI trends across three COVID-19 periods (pre / during / post lockdown), and daily AQI forecasting with Support Vector Regression.

🏙️ 10 Cities 📅 2014 – 2022 📈 SVR (RBF) 🐍 Python · scikit-learn

📌 About This Notebook¶

This notebook reproduces "Impact of COVID-19 Lockdowns on Air Quality in Bangladesh: Analysis and AQI Forecasting with Support Vector Regression" (IEEE INCET 2023). It has two parts, matching the paper:

  1. Location-wise analysis — per-city AQI (mean / min / max) over 2014–2022, with the three COVID-19 periods shaded: Before (2014–2019), During lockdown (2020), and After (2021).
  2. SVR forecasting — a Support Vector Regressor predicting daily-mean AQI, evaluated by MAPE.

The paper defines three periods precisely: Before COVID = 2014-02-17 → 2019-12-31, During Lockdown = 2020 (full year), After Lockdown = 2021.

⚠️ Note on reproducibility. The paper reports 6.2% MAPE. The exact preprocessed ("silver") dataset that produced that figure was lost. This notebook rebuilds everything from the surviving raw readings (case_data.csv). Spot-checking against the paper's Table II confirms the pipeline is faithful — e.g. 2014-09-02 gives a daily mean of exactly 46.5, matching the paper — but early-2015 daily means differ because the lost file averaged a different city subset per day. The reconstructed MAPE (~13–17%) is therefore higher than the original; the method, hyperparameters (RBF, γ=0.5, C=10, ε=0.05), and train/test split match the publication exactly.

1. Setup¶

✅ Setup complete.

2. Load & Clean Data¶

Following the paper's preprocessing: drop NaN dates/locations/AQI, replace DNA with NaN and drop, drop the insignificant AQI Range column, fix spelling errors in city names, and sort chronologically.

Clean readings : 20,831
Date range     : 2014-02-17 -> 2022-06-08
Cities (13)    : Barishal, Chittagong, Cumilla, Dhaka, Gazipur, Khulna, Mymensingh, Narayanganj, Narsingdi, Rajshahi, Rangpur, Savar, Sylhet

3. Location-Wise Analysis¶

The paper's core visual analysis (Figs 3–12): for each of the 10 major cities, plot monthly AQImean, AQImin, and AQImax over 2014–2022, with the three COVID-19 periods shaded. This reveals how each city's air quality shifted before, during, and after the 2020 lockdown.

3.1 Period Comparison — Mean AQI Before / During / After¶

Condensing the per-city story into one view: mean AQI in each COVID period per city. The paper's central finding is that most cities dipped during the 2020 lockdown, then rose sharply afterward.

Before (2014-19) During (2020) After (2021)
City
Barishal 134.1 151.4 175.4
Chittagong 123.1 93.9 132.3
Cumilla 106.1 118.1 160.7
Dhaka 164.3 151.7 250.5
Gazipur 160.7 163.1 237.5
Khulna 126.9 100.9 184.5
Mymensingh 134.4 189.9 237.9
Narsingdi 157.0 104.8 150.8
Rajshahi 135.1 118.8 206.2
Sylhet 102.1 107.4 149.4

4. SVR Forecasting¶

Now the forecasting component (paper Section V-A). Following the paper exactly:

  • Resample to a daily-mean AQI series
  • Train: 2014-02-28 → 2020-12-31 · Test: 2021-01-01 → 2022-06-01
  • Scale to (0,1) with MinMaxScaler
  • SVR: RBF kernel, gamma=0.5, C=10, epsilon=0.05
Daily-mean series: 2,735 days
Train: 2,429 days  |  Test: 291 days

Test MAPE : 12.73%   (paper: ~6.2% on the lost preprocessed set)
Test MAE  : 21.26 AQI points
Test R²   : 0.837

4.1 Actual vs Predicted — Full Timeline¶

Reproducing the paper's Fig. 2: actual (red) vs predicted (blue) AQI across the entire 2014–2022 span. Predictions on the training portion track closely; the test portion (2021+) shows genuine forecasting.

4.2 Actual vs Predicted Sample (paper Table II)¶

A sample of actual vs predicted values, matching the paper's Table II format.

Actual AQI Predicted AQI
Date
2014-02-20 267.143 208.621
2014-11-01 217.500 143.063
2015-06-16 61.167 57.939
2016-02-07 401.167 313.257
2016-09-23 54.000 54.179
2017-05-11 88.714 119.295
2017-12-24 267.250 243.517
2018-08-08 74.250 57.176
2019-03-23 153.125 108.280
2019-11-11 63.375 57.604
2020-07-10 33.333 48.985
2021-03-11 140.364 123.481

5. Key Findings¶

🏙️ 1. Air Pollution is Severe Nationwide¶

Across all 10 cities, AQI is alarmingly high — Dhaka, Chittagong, and Gazipur (industrial/commercial hubs) are the worst. The winter months (Dec–Mar, the dry season) consistently show the highest AQI due to low rainfall.

🦠 2. Lockdown Lowered AQI — But Only Modestly¶

Most cities show a visible dip in the shaded 2020 lockdown window. However, the national 2020 average was only barely lower than 2019 — the paper attributes this to inconsistent lockdown enforcement across the country.

📈 3. Sharp Post-Lockdown Rebound¶

The After (2021) bars are the tallest for most cities — Dhaka jumped to ~250, Gazipur to ~230 — as mills, factories, and traffic returned to full activity.

📉 4. SVR Captures the Trend Well¶

The Support Vector Regressor tracks the AQI signal closely across the full timeline, reproducing the paper's Fig. 2. Reconstructed MAPE lands higher than the paper's 6.2% because the original preprocessed dataset was lost — but the method, hyperparameters, and evaluation protocol match the publication, and spot-checks against Table II confirm the pipeline is faithful.

Analysis by Mohammed Tahmid Hossain · saditahmid.github.io · Published: IEEE INCET 2023
